script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQM recap. The portfolio closed two point three two percent down on the session. It was a punishing day for semiconductor names, and the damage was concentrated, deep, and not evenly distributed across the book.

Micron Technology dropped thirteen point two two percent in a single session. That is not a rounding error — that is a position being repriced in real time, and it dragged the memory complex down with it.

The Nasdaq fell sharply on Thursday as chip stocks led a broad technology selloff for the second consecutive session. TSMC's capital expenditure outlook, reported earlier in the week, raised fresh concerns among analysts about near-term margin compression across the AI infrastructure buildout. No major macro data point was available to offset the pressure, and the index bore the full weight of the semiconductor decline.

The top story Thursday belongs to the memory chip complex, and Micron Technology sits at the center of it. Shares closed at eight hundred fifty-three dollars and twenty cents, down one hundred twenty-nine dollars and ninety-two cents — a loss of thirteen point two two percent on the session. The catalyst traces back to TSMC's capital expenditure guidance, which spooked institutional holders across the AI supply chain. The concern is straightforward: heavier spending by the foundry leader implies longer payback cycles on infrastructure investment and raises questions about whether current AI chip demand forecasts are already priced too optimistically. Micron, which according to reporting from Yahoo Finance supplies fifty-six percent of the world's AI memory chips, is acutely exposed to any shift in sentiment around AI buildout timelines. A company with that kind of market share concentration is both a beneficiary when the cycle accelerates and a target when it stalls. At five point seven five percent of the portfolio, Thursday's move was a meaningful contributor to the session's overall decline. The story inside Micron's Boise headquarters, covered separately by Yahoo Finance, underscores how central this company has become to the United States semiconductor supply chain — but supply chain significance and near-term price momentum are two different things, and Thursday, the market made that distinction clearly.

Turning to Nvidia, which closed at two hundred seven dollars and forty cents, down two point zero eight percent. Nvidia's decline was more contained than others in the semiconductor space, but it was still negative on a session that also saw analyst noise around an income-focused Nvidia exchange-traded fund drawing scrutiny for its structural costs. The broader chip selloff, rather than any company-specific headline, appears to have weighed on the name. The position remains the largest in the portfolio at seven point six zero percent — watch for any commentary from TSMC or downstream AI infrastructure players in the coming sessions.

Apple closed at three hundred thirty-three dollars and twenty-six cents, up five point eight four percent — the standout gainer in Thursday's session and a meaningful offset to the chip damage. Warren Buffett reaffirmed Apple as one of his favored holdings even as CEO Tim Cook prepares to step down, according to Yahoo Finance reporting. The Oracle of Omaha's continued conviction appears to have given institutional holders a reason to add. Apple's so-called Standard Oil strategy, as described in separate analyst commentary cited by Yahoo Finance, is being credited for driving shares to all-time highs. At six point eight zero percent of the portfolio, that gain mattered.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-four dollars and forty-six cents, down one point four zero percent. The name was caught in the AI selloff narrative Thursday, appearing directly in headlines linking the Nasdaq tumble to declines in AI-related stocks. At six point one eight percent of the portfolio, the move was modest but directionally consistent with the session's tech pressure.

Microsoft closed at four hundred one dollars and ten cents, up four point two zero percent. The gain was a notable divergence from the semiconductor names and suggests rotation within the technology sector toward software and cloud. No company-specific catalyst was identified, but the move aligns with the pattern of investors moving away from AI hardware exposure and into platform software names. At four point five two percent of the portfolio, it was a constructive session for Microsoft.

Over at Amazon, shares closed at two hundred forty-nine dollars and eighty-nine cents, up zero point nine seven percent. A quiet but positive session for the consumer discretionary and cloud name. No specific headline drove the move. Amazon continues to benefit from its diversified revenue base, which provides some insulation from pure-play chip sentiment swings.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred dollars and ninety-four cents, down eight point six one percent — the second-worst primary holding performance on the session behind Micron. The decline of forty-seven dollars and nineteen cents reflects direct exposure to the same TSMC capex-driven AI sentiment reset that hit Micron. At three point eight three percent of the portfolio, the damage was meaningful.

Tesla closed at three hundred ninety-one dollars and six cents, down one point two nine percent. No specific company news drove the move Thursday. The consumer discretionary name tracked the broader market lower, though its decline was contained relative to the chip names. Upcoming production data and any commentary around energy storage deployments remain the key catalysts to monitor.

Intel closed at ninety-six dollars and ninety-eight cents, down ten point zero zero percent. That is a ten-point loss in a single session, matching the broader pressure on legacy semiconductor names. Intel's position at two point nine zero percent of the portfolio still made this decline a contributor to Thursday's overall loss. The name has been in a structural transition, and sessions like Thursday illustrate how quickly sentiment can shift when the sector trades as a block.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any follow-through commentary from TSMC or its peer foundries regarding capital expenditure timelines and AI demand signals — this is the direct driver of Thursday's memory and chip selloff and any revision, up or down, will move Micron and Advanced Micro Devices. Second, monitor the SpaceX Starship launch situation — the scrubbed launch reported Thursday evening by Yahoo Finance puts near-term attention on the program's timeline, with the name carrying one point three zero percent of the portfolio. Third, watch for any retail or consumer spending data prints due Friday morning that could shift sentiment in Amazon and Apple, both of which have consumer exposure at meaningful portfolio weights.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. The key question heading into Friday is whether the memory chip selloff stabilizes or whether TSMC capex concerns continue to reprice the AI hardware complex lower.
